disease.name: Hepatitis B
disease.note: Hepadnavirus (HBV) causing acute and chronic hepatitis. Chronic infection risk inversely related to age at acquisition: 90% perinatal, 50% age 1-5, <5% adult. Hepatitis B has been adapted to the hepatology context.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Acute liver failure -> EMERGENCY ICU; Acute hepatitis with jaundice -> OBSERVE; Chronic active hepatitis -> URGENT treatment; Inactive carrier -> SURVEILLANCE

historyFeatures.note: Prodromal serum sickness-like illness with rash and arthritis

examFeatures.note: Chronic HBV: spider angiomas, palmar erythema, caput medusae

subtypes.name: Acute Hepatitis B
subtypes.note: Incubation 60-150 days. Prodrome of arthralgia, rash, then jaundice. Fulminant in <1%. 90% of immunocompetent adults clear spontaneously.

subtypes.name: Chronic Hepatitis B, HBeAg-positive
subtypes.note: High replication phase: elevated HBV DNA >20,000 IU/mL, elevated ALT, HBeAg-positive. High risk of progressive fibrosis.

subtypes.name: Chronic Hepatitis B, HBeAg-negative
subtypes.note: Precore/core promoter mutants. HBV DNA 2,000-20,000 IU/mL, fluctuating ALT. More common in Mediterranean/Middle East. Also progressive.

subtypes.name: Inactive HBsAg Carrier
subtypes.note: HBsAg+, normal ALT, HBV DNA <2,000 IU/mL, HBeAg-negative. Minimal fibrosis. Low risk of progression but HCC risk remains elevated (0.1-0.2%/year).

subtypes.name: Hepatitis B Reactivation
subtypes.note: Rise in HBV DNA in previously inactive carrier or resolved HBV, typically during immunosuppression (chemo, anti-CD20, steroids, TNF-alpha inhibitors).

bedside.name: Urine dipstick for bilirubin and urobilinogen
bedside.indication: detect conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (bilirubinuria); urobilinogen absent in obstruction

laboratory.name: HBsAg (hepatitis B surface antigen)
laboratory.indication: screening test, defines active infection; positive >6 months = chronic

laboratory.name: Anti-HBs (HBsAb)
laboratory.indication: immunity from vaccination or resolved infection

laboratory.name: Total anti-HBc and IgM anti-HBc
laboratory.indication: IgM positive = acute/recent infection; total IgG positive = past exposure or chronic

laboratory.name: HBeAg and anti-HBe
laboratory.indication: HBeAg positivity indicates high replication; HBeAg negative with high DNA suggests precore mutant

laboratory.name: HBV DNA quantitative (PCR)
laboratory.indication: viral load quantification; treatment decision threshold >2,000 IU/mL; monitoring q3-6m on therapy

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin total/direct, ALP, GGT)
laboratory.indication: liver injury assessment; ALT >2x ULN indicates active hepatitis

laboratory.name: INR/PTT
laboratory.indication: synthetic function; prolonged INR in acute liver failure

laboratory.name: Anti-HDV antibody
laboratory.indication: screen for hepatitis D coinfection in HBsAg+ patients

laboratory.name: HIV serology
laboratory.indication: check for coinfection given shared risk factors

laboratory.name: AFP
laboratory.indication: HCC surveillance in chronic HBV (consider US + AFP q6m)

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: liver morphology, steatosis, cirrhosis, HCC screening in chronic HBV

imaging.name: Transient elastography (FibroScan)
imaging.indication: non-invasive fibrosis quantification; guide treatment urgency and prognosis

imaging.name: CT abdomen triphasic or MRI
imaging.indication: HCC diagnosis if mass detected on surveillance US

imaging.name: Liver biopsy
imaging.indication: reserved for discordant non-invasive tests, suspected co-pathology, or clinical trials

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Hepatitis B; EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Hepatitis B

pathways.criteria: IgM anti-HBc+, HBsAg+, elevated ALT 5-20x ULN, acute jaundice/symptoms
pathways.treatment: Supportive care. Antiviral therapy NOT indicated in most acute cases (90% adults clear spontaneously). Monitor LFTs and serology for HBsAg seroconversion. Hospitalize if severe (INR >1.5, bilirubin >10, encephalopathy). Notify public health. Vaccinate household contacts.
Urgent ICU admission. Entecavir 0.5mg PO daily or tenofovir 300mg PO daily. NAC may be considered (limited evidence). ICP monitoring if encephalopathy. Liver transplant evaluation and listing.

pathways.criteria: HBsAg+ >6 months, HBeAg+, HBV DNA >20,000 IU/mL, ALT >2x ULN, and/or fibrosis ≥F2
pathways.treatment: First-line: entecavir 0.5mg PO daily, tenofovir disoproxil fumarate 300mg PO daily, or tenofovir alafenamide 25mg PO daily. Monitor ALT, HBV DNA q3m until undetectable, then q6m. Monitor HBeAg seroconversion q6m. HCC surveillance US + AFP q6m indefinitely.

pathways.criteria: HBsAg+ >6 months, HBeAg-negative, HBV DNA >2,000 IU/mL, ALT >2x ULN, and/or fibrosis ≥F2
pathways.treatment: Same first-line antivirals as HBeAg-positive. Duration: indefinite (relapse common after stopping). Monitor ALT and HBV DNA q3-6m. HCC surveillance.

pathways.criteria: HBsAg+, normal ALT x3 over 6-12 months, HBV DNA <2,000 IU/mL, HBeAg-negative
pathways.treatment: No antiviral therapy currently indicated. Monitor ALT and HBV DNA q6-12 months. HCC surveillance US + AFP q6-12m (risk persists even without cirrhosis). Reassess treatment eligibility if ALT becomes elevated or DNA rises.

pathways.criteria: HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+ (resolved) undergoing immunosuppressive therapy (chemo, rituximab, high-dose steroids, TNF-alpha inhibitors)
pathways.treatment: Prophylactic antiviral: entecavir 0.5mg PO daily or tenofovir. Start before immunosuppression, continue for 6-12 months after completion (12-18 months for rituximab). Monitor HBV DNA monthly during therapy. For resolved HBV (HBsAg-, HBcAb+), monitor ALT and HBV DNA; start antivirals if DNA detectable.

pathways.criteria: Pregnant woman, HBsAg+, HBV DNA >200,000 IU/mL (or high viremia)
pathways.treatment: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate 300mg PO daily from 28-32 weeks gestation until delivery. Infant: HBIG 0.5mL IM within 12 hours of birth + first dose of HBV vaccine. Complete vaccine series. No contraindication to breastfeeding on tenofovir.

disease.emergencyPriority: high in acute liver failure; medium for active chronic; low for inactive carrier
